quickInsight: God didn't just give the Ten Commandments as a list of rules — He gave them because He'd already rescued Israel, and He wanted to show them how to stay free.
keyThemes: You don't have to do everything alone — asking for help is wisdom, not weakness (Exodus 18)
God invites us to covenant with Him — it's a two-way relationship, not a one-sided demand (Exodus 19)
The Ten Commandments are about freedom, not restriction — they protect what matters most
Preparing yourself to receive revelation — being spiritually ready when God speaks (Exodus 19:10-11)
Leadership means serving others and sharing the load (Exodus 18:17-23)
discussionQuestions: Moses was burning out trying to do everything himself until Jethro stepped in (Exodus 18:17-18). When have you tried to handle everything on your own and what happened? Why is it so hard for guys to ask for help?

Before giving the Ten Commandments, God reminded Israel: 'I brought you out of Egypt' (Exodus 20:2). Why do you think He started with what He'd already done for them instead of jumping straight to the rules?
If you had to explain the Ten Commandments to someone who'd never heard of them — without sounding preachy — how would you describe what they're really about?
The Israelites had to prepare for three days before they could approach Mount Sinai (Exodus 19:10-11). What does 'preparing yourself' to hear from God look like for a teenager in 2026?
Which of the Ten Commandments do you think is the hardest for people your age to keep — and which one do you think would make the biggest difference if everyone actually lived it?
teachingIdeas: Start class by giving one person an impossible task alone (stack 20 cups in 30 seconds, solve a puzzle, etc.), then let the whole quorum help. Connect it to Jethro telling Moses he'd wear himself out doing everything solo (Exodus 18). Ask: where in your life are you trying to be a one-man team?

Write the Ten Commandments on the board but frame them as 'What is God protecting?' instead of 'What can't I do?' — for example, 'Thou shalt not steal' protects trust and property; 'Honour thy father and mother' protects families. Let the guys figure out what each commandment is really guarding.

Show a short clip of a coach giving a pregame speech or a captain rallying a team. Compare it to Exodus 19:4-6 where God basically says 'Here's what I've done for you, here's what we can be together.' God's pitch to Israel sounds more like a team captain than a dictator — and He gave them a choice.

Have each young man pick the one commandment they think the world needs most right now and share why. No wrong answers — just let them reason through it. You'll be surprised how thoughtful they get when you let them own the conversation.
activities: Leadership relay challenge: set up an activity where one person has to delegate tasks to the rest of the quorum to accomplish a goal (like building something, completing a scavenger hunt, or running an obstacle course). Debrief with Exodus 18 — what made the leader effective? What made it hard to trust others with your part of the job?

Ten Commandments for our quorum: as a group, come up with 5-10 'quorum commitments' — not church rules, but standards they set for themselves about how they'll treat each other, show up, and have each other's backs. Write them on a poster and have everyone sign it. Connect it to Israel covenanting with God at Sinai.

Service blitz: pick a simple service project the quorum can knock out in one activity night — yard work for a neighbor, assembling care kits, cleaning up a park. Tie it to Exodus 19:5-6 where God calls Israel 'a kingdom of priests' — priests serve. That's who we're becoming.
